metadata.title: Pick a Lane: Find the ONE Real Job (10 pts)
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — PICK A LANE (10 PTS)
metadata.playlist: Plan It Like a PM — Your Madison PRD
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 2 brief — Plan Your Madison Project Like a Pro

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 1. You cannot plan a project that gets you hired if you haven't named the job. Not a job type — an actual posting with an actual company and an actual link. Today we put that on the Figma board. By the end of this video you have one real job posting, the top three technical requirements in the company's own words, and a one-sentence statement of why this role in six to twelve months. That's the whole job. Let's go.

props.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — PICK A LANE (10 PTS)
props.segment: Pick a Lane: Find the ONE Real Job
props.command: Find one real job posting. Extract requirements. Put it on the board.
props.runningText: extracting real requirements from a real posting…
output: Top 3 technical requirements — from their words, not yours

beats.act: what goes in the Dream-Job card
beats.narration_text: The Dream-Job card has four things on it, and all four are required. First: the company name and the exact job title — not 'some marketing role,' the exact title from the posting, word for word. Second: a link or screenshot of the posting, because job postings disappear. Your instructor needs to verify what you were working from, and a dead link is the same as no link. Third: the top three technical requirements the posting explicitly lists — in the company's own language, not paraphrased. If they wrote 'proficiency in SQL for campaign analytics,' that is what goes on the card, not 'data skills.' Fourth: one sentence, six to twelve words, on why this role in six to twelve months. Not 'I think it would be interesting.' A real reason that anchors the rest of your PRD.

artifactLines: 1. Exact job title + company name — word for word from the posting, no paraphrase.
2. Link or screenshot of the posting — postings disappear; your instructor needs to verify.
3. Top 3 technical requirements — the posting's exact words, not your summary of them.
4. One sentence: why this role in 6–12 months — a real reason, not 'I think it sounds interesting.'

beats.act: how to find the right posting
beats.narration_text: Here is the search strategy. Go to LinkedIn, Indeed, or the company's own careers page — not a job aggregator that may be listing outdated roles. Use this prompt to get Claude to orient you. Paste it exactly, filling in your real level and your target domain. The key discipline here: do not accept the first result. When Claude surfaces five posting titles, go find the actual postings on the real job boards and read them. AI summaries of job requirements are sometimes wrong — not because Claude is guessing, but because training data for recent postings can lag. The source of truth is the posting itself, not a summary of it.

props.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — PICK A LANE (10 PTS)
props.command: I'm a [current level — e.g., 'graduate student in AI/marketing'] who wants to work in [domain — e.g., 'AI-powered marketing']. Find me 5 real job posting titles at [target company type] that I could realistically apply to in 12 months. For each, list the top 3 technical requirements.
props.runningText: finding real postings in your target domain…
output: 5 posting titles with company types and realistic fit notes
Top 3 technical requirements per posting — verify each against the actual listing
Then: pick one posting, go find it live, and paste the full text for extraction

beats.narration_text: Once you have the real posting open, paste the full text into Claude with this extraction prompt. The key instruction is in line two: use the posting's exact words for the technical requirements, not a paraphrase. This matters because the gap analysis in Episode 2 maps from these exact phrases. If you paraphrase now, you're doing the gap analysis against something you invented rather than against what the company actually wrote. The extraction prompt also asks for reporting structure if stated — that goes in the 'why this role' sentence and helps you frame the Madison project in the Episode 3 PRD.

props.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — PICK A LANE (10 PTS)
props.command: Here is a real job posting: [PASTE FULL TEXT]. Extract: (1) the exact job title, (2) the top 3 technical requirements the posting explicitly lists — use their exact words, not a paraphrase, (3) the likely team size and reporting structure if stated.
output: Exact job title as written in the posting
Top 3 technical requirements — verbatim from the posting
Team size and reporting structure (if stated) for the Why sentence

beats.act: the excellence move — hiring manager context
beats.narration_text: Here is the excellence move that separates an eight from a ten. Find the likely hiring manager for the role. Knowing their background lets you frame the Madison project in language their team actually uses. Here is how to do it. Search LinkedIn for the company name plus the team name from the posting. Look for someone with 'Head of,' 'Director of,' or 'Lead' in their title whose area matches the role. Note two things: their career background, and any recent content they have posted publicly. Then put the name and a one-sentence context note on the Figma board. Not so you can contact them — so you can calibrate. A PRD written in the hiring manager's vocabulary signals that you did real research, not a template fill.

props.artifactHeading: The excellence move — hiring manager context
artifactLines: Search LinkedIn: [company name] + [team name from posting] — find the Head of / Director of / Lead.
Note their career background — what did they do before this role?
Note any recent public content — what problems are they talking about?
Add to Figma board: name + one-sentence context note.
Purpose: calibrate your PRD vocabulary to their team's language — not for outreach.
props.sparkLine: Calibrate to their vocabulary, not a template.

beats.narration_text: Four traps that show up in nearly every first submission. Trap one: 'Software Engineer somewhere' — that is a job type, not a posting. No company, no link, no requirements, no project direction. Start over. Trap two: no link and no screenshot. The posting will disappear — it may already be gone by the time your instructor grades this. A dead link or a missing screenshot means the card cannot be verified. Trap three: three vague requirements like 'good communication skills' and 'team player.' Those are soft skills. The gap analysis in Episode 2 requires technical requirements — specific tools, platforms, techniques. If the posting you chose only lists soft skills, you chose the wrong posting. Trap four: no reason sentence. 'I want to work there' is not an anchor for a semester project. Write the real reason in one concrete sentence.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Job type, not a posting: 'Software Engineer somewhere' has no company, link, or requirements.
Trap 2 — No link or screenshot: postings disappear; an unverifiable card cannot be graded.
Trap 3 — Soft skills only: 'communication' and 'team player' are not technical requirements; find a posting that lists real tools.
Trap 4 — No reason sentence: 'I want to work there' is not a project anchor — write one concrete sentence.
props.sparkLine: One real posting. All four elements.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Pick a Lane. One real posting — not a job type, a specific posting at a specific company with a live link or screenshot. The exact job title in the company's own words. The top three technical requirements verbatim — not paraphrased. One sentence on why this role in six to twelve months. And the excellence move: a one-sentence hiring manager context note on the Figma board. That is what a ten looks like. The Madison project you build in this course starts here — if the posting is vague, the project will be too. Before you move to Episode 2, confirm the assignment deadline with your instructor: the brief header says May 22, 2026, and the submission block says January 23, 2026 — those do not match, and you need the real date.

props.artifactTitle: Pick a Lane: Find the ONE Real Job
artifactLines: One real posting: specific company, specific title, live link or screenshot.
Exact job title — the posting's words, not your paraphrase of them.
Top 3 technical requirements — verbatim from the posting.
One sentence: why this role in 6–12 months — a real reason, not a preference.
Excellence: hiring manager name + one-sentence context note on the Figma board.
Deadline conflict in the brief (May 22 vs. Jan 23) — confirm the real date with your instructor.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open LinkedIn or Indeed right now. Find one real posting you could realistically apply to in twelve months — a role that exists today at a company you have actually heard of. Copy the full text of the posting. Then paste it into Claude with this extraction prompt. Fill in every placeholder with real values before you submit it. When the output comes back, do not trust the requirements list without checking it against the actual posting text — read the posting and confirm that those exact phrases appear. If they do not, copy the correct phrases from the posting itself. That is how you protect the gap analysis in Episode 2 from being built on a hallucination.

props.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — PICK A LANE (10 PTS)
props.segment: Pick a Lane: Find the ONE Real Job
props.command: Here is a real job posting: [PASTE FULL POSTING TEXT]. Extract: (1) the exact job title as written, (2) the company name, (3) the top 3 technical requirements the posting explicitly states — use their exact words, not a paraphrase, (4) the likely team size and reporting structure if stated, (5) suggest one sentence (6–12 words) for why this role in 12 months based on the posting's context.
props.runningText: fact-check every requirement against the actual posting — AI can hallucinate job requirements…

beats.narration_text: Pick a Lane: Find the ONE Real Job. Liam, in for Bear.
